title: Eight facts about air conditioning amid an overheated global debate - Carbon Brief
gist: Carbon Brief's deep-dive rebuts the 'Europe hates AC' culture-war narrative pushed by figures like Elon Musk and UK/French right-wing politicians, showing Europe's low historic AC adoption stems from a historically cooler climate rather than green ideology, that AC use and sales are rising fast as heat records fall, that AC's climate and urban-heat costs are real but manageable and power-mix dependent, and that UK 'net-zero blocks AC' claims are largely false.

themes.theme: Europe's historical lack of AC need

insights.claim: AC penetration remains very low in northern Europe: only about 6% of German households and 4% of English households have air conditioning.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Extremely low current AC penetration in Germany and England implies a large potential addressable market for AC and heat-pump manufacturers as heat records continue to rise.
insights.quote: 6% of households in Germany and just 4% in England use AC
insights.locator: Much of Europe has not needed AC in the past

insights.claim: Carbon Brief's own analysis of Copernicus ERA5 data shows the average number of days per year above 30C in major European capitals such as London and Paris has jumped sharply since around 2000, explaining why homes built earlier were not designed for cooling.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A quantified, recent (post-2000) step-change in hot days in major European cities is the core empirical basis for reframing rising AC demand as climate-driven rather than cultural.
insights.quote: Capitals such as London and Paris have seen a significant jump since around 2000.
insights.locator: The chart below shows the average number of days per year

insights.claim: Energy researcher Jan Rosenow argues that for most of the 20th century northern Europe did not need cooling because homes in Britain and Germany were built to retain heat, not exclude it, given cold winters and rarely hot summers.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Building stock designed for heat retention rather than cooling is a structural, multi-decade retrofit challenge as European summers heat up, implying sustained demand for retrofit insulation and cooling upgrades.
insights.quote: For most of the 20th century, northern Europe simply didn’t need cooling. Homes in Britain and Germany were built to keep heat in, not out, because winters were cold and summers rarely hot.
insights.locator: Prof Jan Rosenow, an energy and climate researcher at the University of Oxford, tells Carbon Brief

insights.claim: Rosenow contends that European attitudes toward AC are downstream of historical climate, not the reverse — countering the popular narrative that cultural or ideological resistance explains low AC adoption.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: This reframes a widely repeated 'European cultural resistance to AC' narrative as causally backwards, which matters for how policymakers and commentators should message AC rollout rather than assume ideological resistance to overcome.
insights.quote: Attitudes formed around those facts, not the other way round…There is a cultural element, but it is the product of climate, not of some green ideological project.
insights.locator: Rosenow says people’s views on AC in these countries likely stem from their historically colder climates

insights.claim: With Europe now around 2.5C warmer than pre-industrial levels, the UK's Climate Change Committee says societies were 'built for a climate that no longer exists,' forcing a reappraisal of cooling needs even in northern Europe.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Official UK government advisory language framing existing building stock as obsolete for the current climate supports a case for accelerated retrofit and cooling-adaptation policy and spending.
insights.quote: built for a climate that no longer exists
insights.locator: This is forcing a reappraisal of societies

themes.theme: AC adoption masks a sharp north-south divide

insights.claim: The commonly cited figure that 'only about 20%' of European households have AC obscures wide regional variation, with the US (over 90% AC penetration) and Japan the global outliers on the high end.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Continent-wide AC statistics are misleading for market sizing; investors and policymakers should look at country- and region-level penetration rather than EU-wide averages.
insights.quote: more than 90% of households have AC installed
insights.locator: AC is already widely used in hotter parts of Europe

insights.claim: AC is already common in southern Europe: nearly 60% of Italian households have AC, with estimates of 70-80% in Greece and 41% in Spain (with higher penetration in Spain's hotter south).
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Southern European AC markets are already largely saturated or near-saturated, meaning growth capital should focus on northern Europe and on efficiency/replacement upgrades in the south.
insights.quote: nearly 60% of Italian households have AC
insights.locator: Government figures show that nearly 60% of Italian households have AC

insights.claim: France shows a stark internal divide: while a national 2020 figure suggests only 25% of French households have AC, polling data indicates at least 60% of households in Mediterranean regions have it, versus often below 5% in northern regions including Paris.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: National AC statistics for France mask a >10x regional gap, meaning national policy debates about AC 'resistance' misrepresent the real growth opportunity concentrated in northern France.
insights.quote: At least 60% of households in Mediterranean regions of France are equipped with AC
insights.locator: The same pattern can be seen within France

themes.theme: AC politicization and rapidly rising adoption

insights.claim: Elon Musk has promoted an AI-generated message arguing Europeans 'should just install AC,' part of a broader culture-war framing of AC by legacy newspapers, bloggers and right-wing parties including France's National Rally and the UK Conservatives.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: High-profile amplification of the AC culture-war narrative accelerates political salience of the issue, likely pressuring European governments toward faster AC-friendly policy regardless of the underlying climate-driven explanation.
insights.quote: should just install AC
insights.locator: Legacy newspapers, bloggers and even Elon Musk

insights.claim: During the June 2026 heatwave, Germany, France and the UK all saw reports of 'skyrocketing' AC sales, a surge significant enough to be acknowledged by China's foreign ministry given China's role as a major AC supplier.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A sudden, heatwave-triggered AC sales surge across three major European economies, acknowledged even by Chinese officials, is a concrete near-term demand signal for AC manufacturers and exporters (notably Chinese producers).
insights.quote: reports from Germany, France and the UK of “skyrocketing” AC sales
insights.locator: Amid the June heatwave

insights.claim: Marine Tondelier, leader of the traditionally AC-skeptical French Green party, has shifted position, stating there are places where people 'can't do without AC anymore' — signaling a political convergence toward AC support even on the left.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Cross-political convergence on AC necessity in France reduces the likelihood of sustained regulatory or political resistance to AC rollout, supporting a bullish demand case in France.
insights.quote: there are places where we just can’t do without AC anymore
insights.locator: The shift is taking place in politics as well

insights.claim: France, Spain and the Netherlands each used more than twice as much energy for AC and other space cooling in 2024 as in 2015, while German AC production has risen at least 75% in recent years, with a growing share of German homes now built with AC installed.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A doubling-plus of space-cooling energy use in under a decade across multiple large European economies, plus a >75% rise in German AC production, quantifies a fast-growing European AC market that could accelerate further with continued heatwaves.
insights.quote: using more than twice as much energy for AC and other “space cooling” technologies in 2024 as they did in 2015
insights.locator: Overall, AC has been on the rise across Europe

insights.claim: German polling shows that while people are concerned about AC's environmental impact, the high cost of installing and running it is perceived as a greater barrier to adoption than climate policy itself.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Cost, not climate regulation, is the primary barrier to German AC adoption — implying subsidies or financing programs (rather than deregulation) are the more effective lever to accelerate uptake.
insights.quote: the high costs of installing and running it are perceived as greater barriers
insights.locator: Notably, there is little evidence that “climate policies” are blocking Europeans from installing AC

themes.theme: AC's climate footprint is real but power-mix dependent

insights.claim: Global space cooling (mostly AC) used 2,100 terawatt-hours of electricity in 2022 according to the IEA, responsible for about 1bn tonnes of CO2 — roughly 2.7% of total global CO2 emissions from fossil fuels and industry.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A precise baseline (2,100TWh, ~1bn tonnes CO2, 2.7% of global industrial/fossil CO2) lets investors size AC's current climate footprint against other emissions sources rather than rely on vague alarmist framing.
insights.quote: 2,100 terawatt-hours (TWh) of power globally in 2022
insights.locator: According to the International Energy Agency (IEA), “space cooling”

insights.claim: IEA 2018 estimates project that, if unchecked, global AC energy demand will more than triple by 2050 to 6,200TWh, with households driving 70% of the increase and two-thirds of the world's households potentially owning AC by mid-century.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A tripling of AC electricity demand by 2050, concentrated in household adoption, is a major long-term structural growth driver for global electricity demand and grid buildout, especially in emerging markets.
insights.quote: energy demand from AC will more than triple by 2050
insights.locator: According to the IEA estimates from 2018

insights.claim: Lauri Myllyvirta of the Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air argues alarmist messaging overstates AC's electricity burden: AC currently uses about 1% of EU electricity, and catching up to US adoption rates would only double that share.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A relatively modest ~1-2% EU electricity impact even at full US-level AC adoption suggests AC-driven grid stress fears in Europe may be overstated relative to other demand drivers like AI datacentres or EV charging.
insights.quote: Currently, AC uses about 1% of electricity in the EU and catching up to adoption rates in the US would double this.
insights.locator: In a LinkedIn post, Lauri Myllyvirta

insights.claim: AC's climate impact depends heavily on a country's power generation mix: in France, where nuclear supplies 67% of electricity, expanding AC has a far more limited climate impact than in coal-reliant grids.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Grid decarbonization is the single biggest lever determining whether AC growth adds materially to emissions — low-carbon grids like France's can absorb AC growth with minimal climate cost, a useful screen for country-level climate risk from cooling demand.
insights.quote: nuclear energy accounts for 67% of its electricity generation
insights.locator: For instance, in countries with a low-carbon electricity mix

insights.claim: In India, portable 'room AC' units alone already account for nearly a quarter of the country's peak electricity demand (60-70GW), even though the majority of Indian households have not yet bought their first AC unit, against a coal-heavy grid especially exposed at night.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: India represents the single largest latent AC-driven emissions and grid-stress risk globally — a huge unmet household AC market layered on a coal-heavy, night-vulnerable grid, making Indian grid decarbonization and AC efficiency standards a critical climate variable.
insights.quote: already accounts for nearly one-quarter of India’s peak electricity demand (60-70GW)
insights.locator: According to a new working paper from the India Energy and Climate Center (IECC)

insights.claim: Nikit Abhyankar of the India Energy and Climate Center says AC expansion should be paired with solar and battery storage, noting the economics of that pairing have 'completely shifted' in recent years, cutting both energy bills and emissions.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Pairing AC rollout with solar-plus-storage is presented as an economically favorable mitigation strategy for emerging markets, relevant to solar/storage capex planning in high-growth AC markets like India.
insights.quote: the “economics have completely shifted” in the last few years
insights.locator: Dr Nikit Abhyankar, co-faculty director of the IECC, tells Carbon Brief

insights.claim: The IEA estimates that accelerating energy-efficiency improvements could deliver more than one-third of all CO2 emission reductions needed between now and 2030, and could cut projected 2050 global AC electricity demand by 1,300GW — equivalent to the capacity of all of China and India's coal plants combined.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Efficiency standards for AC units represent one of the single largest available levers for cutting future global emissions and avoided coal capacity — a strong case for regulatory and investment focus on AC efficiency standards over blanket restriction.
insights.quote: reduced by 1,300GW – the equivalent of all of China and India’s coal plants
insights.locator: The global energy demand needed to run ACs alone in 2050

themes.theme: The urban heat island feedback loop

insights.claim: AC works by removing heat from indoor air and expelling it outdoors, directly raising street-level temperatures and worsening the urban heat island effect — a mechanism cited by Paris deputy mayor Emmanuel Grégoire, who called individual AC 'a scourge' that heats the city further.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: AC's local heat-emission externality is a distinct cost from its electricity-driven CO2 footprint, and is being used explicitly by French left-wing politicians to argue for collective rather than individual cooling solutions.
insights.quote: individual AC is a scourge – it makes the problem worse by heating the city even more
insights.locator: Emmanuel Grégoire, the Socialist mayor of Paris

insights.claim: One study finds that in Phoenix, Arizona, where AC use is widespread, AC operation during a heatwave can raise night-time outdoor temperatures by 1-1.5C.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A measured 1-1.5C night-time urban warming effect from widespread AC use is a quantifiable externality relevant to city climate-adaptation and urban-planning cost-benefit analysis.
insights.quote: AC use during a heatwave can raise night-time temperatures by 1-1.5C
insights.locator: One study concludes that, in a city such as Phoenix, Arizona

insights.claim: A model of a nine-day heatwave in Paris under a future of 'massive' AC use finds an increase in external temperature of more than 2C, purely from heat emitted by AC units.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A modeled >2C outdoor temperature rise from future mass AC adoption in Paris implies AC rollout without complementary passive-cooling measures could partly offset its own health benefits at the street level.
insights.quote: an increase in external temperature of more than 2C, due to heat emitted by the units
insights.locator: Another models a nine-day heatwave in Paris

insights.claim: The IPCC has formally concluded that AC may constitute climate 'maladaptation' because of its high energy demand and associated heat emissions, especially in high-density cities.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: An official IPCC 'maladaptation' classification for AC gives regulatory and academic legitimacy to arguments for pairing AC rollout with passive cooling and urban design measures rather than relying on AC alone.
insights.quote: AC may constitute a maladaptation because of its high demands on energy and associated heat emissions, especially in high-density cities.
insights.locator: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) has highlighted this issue

insights.claim: Vincent Viguié of École des Ponts ParisTech argues that Europe's denser urban form (compared to more suburban US cities) leaves Europeans more exposed to AC-driven local heat emissions than Americans.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Higher European urban density means the urban-heat-island cost of mass AC adoption will likely be proportionally larger in Europe than in the US, reinforcing the case for passive cooling investment alongside AC in dense European cities.
insights.quote: there is a key difference between US and European cities
insights.locator: According to Dr Vincent Viguié, a climate change economist at École des Ponts ParisTech

themes.theme: AC saves lives, but isn't the whole answer to heat deaths

insights.claim: World Weather Attribution scientists state that heatwaves cause more deaths in Europe than all other natural hazards combined, and the June 2026 heatwave alone is estimated to have killed more than 20,000 people in Europe, including over 2,700 heat-related deaths in France.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Heatwaves are quantified as Europe's deadliest natural hazard category, with a single 2026 event killing over 20,000 — establishing extreme heat as a top-tier mortality and public-health-spending risk, not a secondary climate impact.
insights.quote: more than 2,700 heat-related deaths
insights.locator: In France – which has seen some of the hottest temperatures

insights.claim: A 2021 study found that AC use globally averted an estimated 190,000 heat-related deaths annually during 2019-21.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A concrete global mortality-reduction figure for AC underpins the public-health case for expanding cooling access, especially in vulnerable, low-AC-penetration populations.
insights.quote: AC averted an estimated 190,000 heat-related deaths annually during 2019-21
insights.locator: AC does help to protect people from the effects of extreme heat

insights.claim: Heat kills around 11 people per 100,000 in Europe compared to about 2 per 100,000 in the US, a gap analyst Hannah Ritchie links to the US's much higher AC penetration, though she cautions that comparisons to US gun deaths are 'a bit silly' since US gun deaths are higher on a per-capita basis.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A roughly 5x heat-mortality gap between Europe and the US, plausibly linked to AC penetration, quantifies the potential public-health upside of closing Europe's AC gap.
insights.quote: Heat kills around 11 people out of every 100,000 in Europe, compared to around two people in the US
insights.locator: Heat kills around 11 people out of every 100,000 in Europe

insights.claim: A 2020 study of Canada, Japan, Spain and the US found that while heat-related mortality fell substantially between 1972 and 2009, increased AC adoption explained only 16.7% of the mortality decline in the US and 14.3% in Spain — meaning other factors did most of the work.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: AC explains less than a fifth of the historical decline in heat mortality in the US and Spain, meaning policy focused solely on AC rollout will likely underdeliver on mortality reduction versus a broader mix of adaptation measures.
insights.quote: an increase in AC only explained 16.7% of the drop in the US and 14.3% in Spain
insights.locator: However, an increase in AC only explained 16.7% of the drop

insights.claim: Measures already rolled out in France since the deadly 2003 heatwave (which killed 70,000 across Europe), including mandatory air-conditioned nursing-home rooms since 2004 and a multi-tiered heat action plan, could cut the projected death toll of a repeat 2003-level heatwave by more than 75%.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A >75% projected mortality reduction from France's post-2003 heat policy package is strong evidence that structured public-health heat plans (not just AC) deliver outsized life-saving returns.
insights.quote: cut the projected death toll of a 2003-like heatwave by more than 75%
insights.locator: Another study found that measures that have already been rolled out in France

themes.theme: UK 'net-zero blocks AC' claims are largely false

insights.claim: Daily Telegraph columnist Matthew Lynn falsely claimed that strict net-zero rules mean AC is 'effectively banned' in the UK, then later conceded in the same article that AC is not literally banned in new-build homes, only harder and more expensive to install.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A self-contradicting, widely circulated claim that net zero bans AC in the UK illustrates how misinformation about climate policy can spread through mainstream right-leaning UK media, shaping public and political sentiment against net zero.
insights.quote: Strict net-zero rules now mean that aircon is effectively banned in the UK.
insights.locator: Writing in the climate-sceptic Daily Telegraph, columnist Matthew Lynn claimed falsely

insights.claim: The UK Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government explicitly rebutted media claims that AC is banned in UK homes, stating the claim is incorrect.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
entities: UK Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government
insights.so_what: An explicit government rebuttal of the 'AC is banned' narrative provides a clear, citable fact-check for policymakers and media navigating the UK's net-zero/AC debate.
insights.quote: There has been media coverage this week suggesting that AC is banned in homes. This is incorrect.
insights.locator: A blog post from the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government rebuts these claims

insights.claim: The London Plan does not ban home AC; it requires that new buildings prioritize passive design measures (building orientation, window design, external shading, trees) before relying on mechanical cooling.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The London Plan's 'passive-first' cooling hierarchy is a design-standard model that could be replicated elsewhere to balance cooling access with reducing urban heat-island and energy impacts.
insights.quote: It simply says that, for new buildings, passive design measures should be prioritised
insights.locator: The London Plan does not stop homes from having AC

insights.claim: New-build homes in England must comply with 'Part O' of the 2022 building regulations, which requires addressing overheating primarily through energy-efficient, passive design, treating AC as a last resort rather than a ban.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: England's building code treats AC as a fallback rather than prohibiting it, meaning regulatory friction for AC is a design-hierarchy issue, not an outright legal barrier — relevant for AC/heat-pump vendors targeting new-build markets.
insights.quote: addressing overheating in buildings through energy-efficient design and prioritising passive cooling, with AC as a last resort
insights.locator: Elsewhere, new-build homes across England must meet the requirements of “part O”

insights.claim: For existing UK buildings, most AC units qualify under 'permitted development rights,' meaning no planning application is required to install them, and 2025 regulatory changes further eased planning requirements for air-to-air heat pumps specifically.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Regulatory easing (not tightening) for AC/heat-pump installation in existing UK homes directly contradicts the 'net-zero blocks AC' narrative and signals a friendlier near-term installation environment for UK heat-pump/AC vendors.
insights.quote: regulations were relaxed in 2025 to make it easier to install an air-to-air heat pump
insights.locator: Additionally, regulations were relaxed in 2025

insights.claim: Andrew Sissons of Nesta says the UK government should implement its announced £2,500 subsidy for air-to-air heat pumps 'as quickly as possible' and further expand permitted development rights, particularly for flats and dense urban areas.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A specific £2,500 UK subsidy for air-to-air heat pumps is a concrete near-term policy catalyst for heat-pump/AC demand that investors should track for implementation timing.
insights.quote: as quickly as possible
insights.locator: Speaking to Carbon Brief, Andrew Sissons, director of sustainable future at Nesta

insights.claim: UK government advisors at the Climate Change Committee estimate that 22% of the UK's housing stock will likely need active cooling such as AC to cope with 2C of global warming, with active cooling more likely needed in retrofits of existing homes than in new builds.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A specific 22% housing-stock figure gives a quantifiable addressable market size for UK AC/active-cooling retrofit demand tied to a defined 2C warming scenario.
insights.quote: 22% of the UK’s housing stock will likely need active cooling
insights.locator: UK government advisors at the Climate Change Committee (CCC) suggest

themes.theme: Beyond AC: passive cooling and behavioral alternatives

insights.claim: France's far-right National Rally party has proposed a 'grand plan for AC' across all public buildings, which Dutch MEP Gerben-Jan Gerbrandy notes comes from the same political actors who have for years delayed broader emissions reductions.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Far-right parties adopting AC as a signature climate-adjacent policy while opposing emissions cuts illustrates how the AC debate is being used as a wedge to reframe climate politics without addressing underlying causes.
insights.locator: As noted by Dutch MEP Gerben-Jan Gerbrandy

insights.claim: Researchers at the Basque Centre for Climate Change warn that framing AC as the 'default solution' to heat risks missing the opportunity to design more inclusive, human-centred cooling responses.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A caution against AC-as-default-solution supports policy design that blends AC with passive and behavioral measures rather than treating cooling as a single-technology problem — relevant to how cities allocate adaptation budgets.
insights.quote: more inclusive, human-centred responses
insights.locator: In a Carbon Brief guest post published in 2025

insights.claim: Aditya Valiathan Pillai of King's College London argues that by centring the heat debate on AC, the far-right response to Europe's heatwaves has 'completely neglected the science of how you cool human beings,' pointing to ceiling fans, openable windows, cross-ventilation and reduced heat-exposure hours as widely used alternatives in hot developing countries.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A researcher explicitly argues that non-AC cooling science (fans, ventilation, exposure reduction) is being sidelined by AC-centric political debate, suggesting underinvestment in lower-cost, scalable adaptation methods relevant to emerging markets.
insights.quote: completely neglected the science of how you cool human beings
insights.locator: King’s College London’s Pillai says

insights.claim: Pillai proposes a temperature-based decision rule: places reaching 42C and above need to seriously consider AC, while places in the low-to-mid 30s Celsius could rely on alternative cooling strategies.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A concrete temperature threshold (42C) for prioritizing AC over alternatives is a practical, actionable framework for climate-adaptation planners deciding where to prioritize AC investment versus passive cooling.
insights.quote: definitely need to think about AC very seriously
insights.locator: Pillai tells Carbon Brief that, while places reaching 42C and higher

insights.claim: Pillai argues that behavioral change is the 'least glamorous part' of heat policy but 'pulls most of the weight' in protecting people, citing measures like lighter clothing, more fluids, and workplace protections such as mandatory breaks, shade requirements, and heat-stress insurance.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Behavioral and workplace-policy interventions are framed as the highest-leverage but most politically unglamorous tools for heat mortality reduction, a lower-capex complement to AC investment that policymakers may be underweighting.
insights.quote: pulls most of the weight
insights.locator: Behavioural change, he adds, is the “least glamorous part” of heat policy
